About Me

I'm Emmanuel Ayodele — a Product Designer, UI/UX Designer and Creative Director with 4+ years of experience building intuitive digital products across mobile and web.

My process runs end-to-end: from user research and wireframing through high-fidelity prototyping and usability testing. I sit at the intersection of design craft and business strategy — translating briefs into solutions that measurably move the needle.

My path here started somewhere unexpected — a Civil Engineering degree from the University of Ilorin. Years of studying load-bearing structures, tolerances, and how thousands of small decisions hold a building together taught me to see systems before I see screens. Today, when I open Figma, I'm still asking an engineer's questions: where's the stress point in this flow? What happens when this "load" — a user, a transaction, a thousand daily logins — hits scale? That structural instinct is why my interfaces don't just look good; they're built to hold weight, which is exactly the kind of confidence that makes clients and hiring teams trust the work.

Beyond design, I'm a passionate mentor actively shaping the next generation of Nigerian UI/UX designers at Ceresense IT Training Institute.

🎯

Selected Work

Products I've shipped

View Dribbble →
Featured Project Product Design Dashboard UX

Loan Management Application — JK Solutn Ltd.

Led end-to-end design of an internal loan management platform, from discovery and user research through to handoff. The system streamlined the entire loan lifecycle — application, approval, disbursement, and repayment tracking — for internal staff.

90%
Staff Adoption
86%
Engagement↑
₦18M
Faster Disbursement

A structured approval pipeline — designed the way an engineer sequences a build, with clear dependencies and no idle stages — cut average loan turnaround time, unlocking an estimated ₦18M in additional loans processed per quarter at no extra staffing cost.

Module Deep-Dive

Wallet Module — Dual-Wallet Incentive System

Within the same platform, I designed and delivered the Wallet Module — a role-based incentive and debt-tracking system for JK Solutn's Customer Service Officers (CSOs). The brief arrived as a Figma design brief and developer handover document. What it required was not just a screen layout but a complete logic-driven UI that made a complex financial mechanic legible to three distinct user roles with three entirely different permission sets.

Wallet A — Main Wallet

Holds performance bonuses (1% per loan after the 100th disbursement), company incentives, and finalized deductions transferred from Wallet B after 90 days. Cleared and paid out by Admin/HR at month-end.

Wallet B — Tracker Wallet

Tracks Balance of Overdue Debt (BOD) per CSO. Each overdue loan remains here for 90 days before auto-converting into a deduction inside Wallet A. Read-only for CSOs; fully editable for Admin/HR.

CSO (Field Officer)

Read-only dashboard — Wallet A summary, Wallet B countdown table, bonus progress bar (92/100 loans), BOD maturity alerts, and a dispute-deduction button for contested entries.

Admin / HR

Full edit access — filter/search CSOs by name or branch, switch between Wallet A and B tabs, post manual bonuses or deductions, trigger the monthly "Clear Wallet A" payroll action, export PDF/CSV.

Manager

View-only with print/export options — oversight without the ability to alter entries, preserving audit integrity across the incentive structure.

Every permission cue, countdown timer, and color state (green = bonus, red = deduction, amber = countdown warning) was mapped from the 90-day conversion logic before a single frame was drawn. The design process here mirrors what I learned in structural engineering: understand the load path before you build the structure. The UI is the structure; the business rule is the load.

Featured Case Study

Redesigning Paga — Security, Accessibility & Trust in Digital Banking

An independent UX audit and full redesign of Nigeria's leading mobile payments app, addressing a critical transaction-security gap, introducing dark mode accessibility, and modernizing the visual system end to end.

RoleProduct Designer (UI/UX)
TypeUX Audit & Mobile Redesign
ToolsFigma
ScopeIndependent Project
Overview

Digital payment apps have become essential infrastructure for everyday financial life — sending money, paying bills, topping up airtime, all expected to happen quickly and safely. After conducting an independent UX review of Paga, one of Nigeria's most widely used mobile payment platforms, I identified meaningful gaps in visual consistency, accessibility, and — most critically — transaction security. This case study walks through that audit, the reasoning behind each design decision, and the redesigned flows that resulted.

Problem Statement
01
No Dark Mode Support

The original app shipped with a single light interface, limiting accessibility for users who need reduced brightness, use their phones in low-light conditions, or simply prefer a different visual mode.

02
Inadequate Transaction Security

The most serious finding: once logged in, a user could complete a financial transfer with no additional authentication step — no PIN, no biometric check. Anyone with momentary access to an unlocked device could move money unverified.

03
Visual & Hierarchy Gaps

Inconsistent component styling, weak visual hierarchy, and dated UI patterns made the experience feel less trustworthy than the financial weight of the product demands.

Research & Design Goals

The redesign was guided by three goals, each tested against real interaction patterns rather than aesthetic preference alone:

◐
Improve Accessibility — introduce a fully designed dark mode that reduces eye strain, improves comfort during extended use, and gives users control over their own viewing experience.
⛨
Strengthen Transaction Security — insert a mandatory authentication layer (PIN or biometric) between transaction review and execution, closing the unverified-transfer gap entirely.
◆
Modernize the Interface — rebuild key screens with clearer hierarchy, consistent components, and a more premium, trustworthy visual language befitting a financial product.

Home screen redesign — progressive states from light to dark mode, with refined visual hierarchy, consistent Quick Access components, and improved use of contrast throughout.

The Secure Transaction Flow

This was the core of the redesign. The original flow allowed money to move with zero friction after login — efficient, but dangerously unverified:

Before
Login→ Select Recipient→ Enter Amount→ Send (Unverified)
After
Login→ Select Recipient→ Enter Amount→ Review→ Choose Verification→ Confirm — PIN or Biometric

Critically, this extra step was designed to add protection without adding friction that punishes legitimate users — the verification screen offers a choice between PIN and biometric confirmation, so the user picks whichever is faster on their device.

Transfer review screen showing full fee and tax breakdown, followed by the new Confirm Transfer step — the user must explicitly choose PIN or Biometric verification before the system proceeds.

Final authentication states — numeric PIN entry, fingerprint biometric prompt, and the success confirmation — closing the loop on a transaction that is now fully verified end to end.

Final Solution
Security
  • Mandatory transaction confirmation step
  • PIN verification option
  • Biometric fingerprint authentication
  • Closed the unauthorized-transfer risk entirely
Accessibility
  • Fully designed dark mode
  • Improved color contrast and readability
  • Greater user control and personalization
  • Reduced eye strain in low-light use
Interface
  • Cleaner, more consistent components
  • Stronger visual hierarchy throughout
  • Modern, premium banking aesthetic
  • Reusable patterns for future screens
Reflection

This project demonstrates how small, strategic design decisions can materially change the risk profile of a financial product — not through complexity, but through deliberate friction placed exactly where it matters. Closing the unverified-transaction gap didn't require a rebuild of Paga's entire architecture; it required identifying precisely where the existing flow failed its users and inserting protection at that single load-bearing point.

That's the same structural instinct I bring from my civil engineering background into every product I design: find the point of failure before it fails, and reinforce exactly there — nowhere else, so the experience stays light everywhere it can and strong exactly where it must.

UX Research UX Audit Problem Identification User-Centered Design Mobile App Design Interaction Design Accessibility Design Design Systems Information Architecture Security-Oriented UX Figma Prototyping

My Process

How I design things

A disciplined, repeatable process that balances speed with depth — because good design is both a craft and a method.

01
Discover

User interviews, stakeholder alignment, competitive analysis, and defining the real problem to solve.

02
Define

Synthesising insights into personas, jobs-to-be-done, and a sharp design brief.

03
Design

Low-fidelity wireframes → component library → high-fidelity screens in Figma with interactive prototypes.

04
Test

Usability testing, design critique, iterating on findings until the experience feels inevitable.

05
Deliver

Dev-ready handoff with annotated specs, a design system, and post-launch metrics tracking.

" My approach will not only put your products and brand ahead of the competition, but make a lasting impression on your customers and target audience.
